Great pitch, but now your audience is tired and they're about to
check out. What you do next will be the difference between
success and failure. We're now in module three of the Strong
Method. This is Reveal the Intrigue.
Let's just take a quick look at where we came from. In the Strong
Method, we had "S", which is set the frame. When you set the
frame, you raised your status high enough to pitch effectively. If
you pitch from a low status position, as we learned, no one's
going to be able to listen to you or pay attention. You've got
people's attention, you prized yourself, you let them know they're
the commodity and what you have is valuable. Money is a
commodity. The deal or ideas or project you have, or the product
is the prize. You've oriented people to the deal.
Then you went into "T", which is tell the story or give the
narrative. You gave people your big idea. You explained the
problem you solved and how you solved it with your solution. You
gave a value proposition, the economics, why do it now, how the
market is changing. You gave a great narrative. It was emotional
and you really captured their attention and made them pay
attention to you for a good period of time.
Now that pitch is over. So, for about 20 minutes, you really had
people's attention. That pitch is over. I'm going to talk about what
happens next. That's "R", reveal the intrigue. A period of focused
attention leads to reduced mental efficiency. We all know this. If
you've captured someone's attention and really had them listen
to you for 20 minutes and now your pitch is over, it's almost like
your car stalled out. You have to restart the whole thing. Take the
key out, put it back in the ignition, get the lights to turn back on,
fire up the motor, get it back in gear, and start it rolling again. I
think it's a good metaphor for what happens.
Your pitch is over. You did a great job. But now your audience is
tired. When people are tired, they are designed to focus their
attention on stimuli that are most likely to be of importance.
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	1
Oren Klaff:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 23 --

Usually, that means being of importance to survival. If you think
about the phases that we're going through in the Strong Method,
if you really want to chunk up, in the pitch, we went and got
attention. Now, in "R", reveal the intrigue, we're going to work
them into getting engagement. After engagement, we're going to
go for decision. These are the phases: attention, we set the frame
and tell the story, engagement is what we're going for now with
"R", reveal the intrigue.
Our brain can't possibly keep up with everything that's happening
in our environment. As we get tired, we get more and more
focused on the things that are relevant. The brain operates at a
very high, fixed cost. It needs to ignore most of what's happening
around us, especially when we get tired. The vast majority of
things aren't meaningful anyway. You know if you had to pay
attention to everything, information overload would fry you out
quickly. Thinking wears you out. We have just asked someone to
focus on us very hard, think very hard about our stuff, and now,
their brain is really going to be slipping into survival mode. After
the pitch or presentation, the audience's brain doesn't want to do
anything, certainly if it doesn't have to. It doesn't matter how well
you have pitched. Some things are going to start happening.
You're going to start getting questions.
I want to just give you a sense now of the limits of human
attention and why you're going to start getting questions. We all
know, give a great pitch and you're going to start getting
questions. I want you to focus on this plus sign in the middle of
the circle. Keep your gaze on it and don't release your gaze from
it. Now, move your attention, but not your gaze, to the lines on
the left. You cannot count them. You can observe them; you can
see they're there. But you cannot count how many there are.
Keep your attention focused on the plus sign with the circle
around it. Keep your gaze, your view, your eyes looking at it. Move
your attention to count the lines on the right. You can count them
easily. So here, you can really feel the limits of human attention. I
can tell you, if you've given a great pitch you are now pushing the
limits of human attention.
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	2
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 23 --

Questions are going to come at you for three reasons. One,
you've left information gaps. It doesn't matter how great you are
at pitching. We all do this. We cause, in people's minds, gaps of
information. So, some questions are just going to come because
you've left information gaps. There is another reason you might
get questions: you've caused a lot of excitement with your pitch.
"Hey, what if we could add your product to our product. Then we
could make something bigger and better". When we raise
excitement you're going to get a lot of "what if" questions. The
third reason, and probably the most concerning reason, is
because you've raised your status so high in your pitch that the
analyst attitude in the room is going to want to catch up to you.
They're going to want to ask questions to point out weaknesses in
your deal.
I always use blue plastic frogs as an example. If I'm pitching blue
plastic frogs and I've raised my status so high by giving a great
pitch, I'm going to get questions trying to lower my status and
make other people look good. "Well, in our experience, the blue
will wear off within a year in rainy environments. Can you prove
that yours doesn't do that," or "In our experience, it's too
expensive to buy blue ones and the yellow or red ones are much
better." People are going to try to make your product or idea or
project look weak with questions because they want to raise their
own status because you've raised yours so high. They feel an
imbalance. Those are the reasons you're going to get questions.
This is where trouble will start. You cannot just start answering
questions. You have to keep a narrative structure. I will explain
what this means now, but the idea of relying on a narrative
structure is a radical change from what most of us have been
taught and conditioned to do over our business encounters. When
someone asks us a question we answer it. We give the facts. But
here's why we have to rely on a narrative structure and not just
facts: facts are not interesting. It's funny. They even call them
factoids to try and give them a cute, green martian name. Facts
aren't interesting. We had to rename them factoids to make them
even sound friendly.
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	3
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 23 --

Answers are not interesting. They're useful, but they're not
interesting. What's most interesting is what's unknown. The art of
the possible is what's interesting. Maybe you can think like a
writer in this way. Writers prize questions more than answers.
Writers and the narrative structure celebrate paradox, mystery
and uncertainty. It recognizes all of them as seeds of connection.
Uncertainty, paradox, mystery and questions: these are seeds of
human connections. Answers are a point of disconnect. The
unknown is a point of connection.
At first, it's hard to recognize how radical a notion this is, but
think about it this way: we're taught in business to process
information, retain it and then deliver it on request immediately.
We are rewarded for providing answers to questions posed by
others. You are rewarded for doing that. But, again, once you give
someone an answer it is a point of disconnect. They have the
information they need and they don't need to pay attention to
you any longer. It may seem like an unusual point of view since
the goal of making a presentation or a pitch is to inform. We have
to give answers. We have to give facts. We have to give people
information. But we have to be careful because the second they
have everything they need, then the presentation is over. They're
going to work to leave.
Typical buyers want data and information at the end of the
presentation to solve the Rubik's cube you've given them. You've
posed a problem. In fact, in "Tell the Story", part of the job there
was to explain the problem you've solved. You posed a problem
and then you proposed a solution for it. They paid a lot of
attention to you and they want a reward for their mental energy
expenditure. They want an answer. It's funny, if you've ever seen
"The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy", they even had to give an
answer for the meaning of life, which I think, in the book, was 42.
Humans are driven for answers. We even had to assign a number
to the meaning of life. That's how driven to answers we are.
If you think about it in your own experience, you know business
people who pay lip service to the notion that they welcome
criticism. You may have had a boss or partner or customer who
said "Hey, we welcome criticism. We welcome opposing points of
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	4
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 23 --

views from others. We want you to challenge our thinking." But
the reality is that people prize flattery. They want parroting of
their own thoughts and ideas back to them. The more powerful
the people are, in the power frame we've come to know and
talked about in "Framing", the more people surround themselves
or even insulate themselves with "yes" people who give them the
instant answers to their questions and the answers they want,
people want answers. They want facts, they want information.
Once you deliver all that information the presentation and even
the meeting is over. This is what we're going to be talking about
here: how to prevent this. The unanswered question prompts a
very strong reaction. Almost panic and anxiety and it makes
people uncomfortable. This may seem like an unusual point of
view since I've said the goal of making a presentation is to give
the information and not to have unanswered questions. The
logical step at the end of the presentation is to provide people all
the facts and information they want, rather than unknowns. The
fundamental paradox of the unknown is that even as most people
are uncomfortable with it, they're very fascinated by it. Curiosity
remains irrepressible in the human spirit.
Let's take a look at where we are and get very specific about some
things to do. Again, we're past setting the frame. We've passed
giving the pitch. We now need to engage with intrigue. Let me say
it this way: today, with the raw competition for people's time, the
presenter, that's you, pitch man, pitch woman, cannot count on
anyone to pay attention out of obligation, moral duty or
dedication to being informed. They will not listen to you, read
what you have or pay attention to you because someone told
them your information is important or because they have to.
People will only listen to you because they want to. They will want
to because they are intrigued and curious. This is why this module
is called "Reveal the Intrigue".
In some ways, intrigue is at odds with human nature to solve
problems and detect patterns. Humans have this phenomenal
ability to detect and interpret really simple patterns. It's what
helped our ancestors survive the hazardous, primeval world. It
enabled them to evade predators, find food and shelter and,
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	5
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 23 --

eventually, to learn to plant crops in the right place at the right
time of year. That's all pattern making. When it comes to business
decisions our search for patterns leads us to assume that order
exists, when often it doesn't.
Humans, I have found, and I think scientists and anything you
read will confirm, are uniquely obsessive about finding patterns.
Our knack or desire or proclivity for perceiving order even when it
isn't there is a problem. I want to show you know. I draw these
myself, so they're not perfect triangles. If you look at these
triangles, these are clearly pointing in the downward left
direction. When we introduce a ninth triangle, it's obviously
pointing in the same direction. At least in my mind, it's very easy
to detect that pattern, but is it? Now, when I introduce another
eight triangles, again, of my own drawing, now I'm confused. The
pattern I built previously seems incorrect. Why am I even building
a pattern here? What do these triangles mean? They mean
nothing. I made them up. They have no meaning. Here we are,
debating. At least I'm debating with myself. Does that triangle
point to the right or does it point downward to the left? These
triangles have no meaning. They're not connected to any decision
making. They don't affect my life in any way yet I'm trying to solve
this eternal debate. Which way is the triangle in the middle
pointing? It's absurd.
Humans are pattern making machines. Why can we spot patterns
where there are none? People think there's a module in the left
hemisphere of the brain that drives humans to search for patterns
and seek causal relationships even when none exist. Researchers
call this part of the brain "The Interpreter". The Interpreter drives
us to believe "I can figure this out." This is probably a good thing
when there's a pattern of data that isn't very complicated. This
constant search for explanations and patterns in random or
complex data is not a good thing. That's the investment
understatement of the century. The financial markets are almost
random and they vary in really complex ways. No one has yet
identified where in the brain this interpreter is located, but it
explains why experts keep trying to predict the unpredictable.
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	6
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 23 --

How does this relate to you? At the end of your presentation, what
happens now? The buyer or your audience leaps to conclusions.
This is pattern making. There is a part of their brain that is driving
them to find a pattern that may not exist in the things you have
said. This is unconscious behavior. It's not something they're
trying to do to thwart you. This is automatic. As I've said here, the
brain will automatically search for patterns. It's how we are built.
It's funny. They do these experiments in rats versus humans. They
feed the same pattern of reward to rats and humans. In humans,
it's money. If you push a button, you get a certain reward. If you
push a different button you get a different reward. If you push a
third button you get yet a different reward. Humans will spend
hours trying to determine the pattern that will get them the most
reward. With the rats, they push a button and they get a certain
amount of food. It's totally random. There is no pattern. Rats, in
less than 10 minutes, figure out that one button gives them food
80% of the time. Humans almost never figure this out. The rats
just push the button over and over again to get the most amount
of food. The humans believe there is some underlying, magic
pattern. In this way, rats are smarter than humans because they
don't chase patterns and they don't look for solutions when there
may not be one.
The search for patterns is uncontrollable. We can't turn this kind
of processing off in our mind. We can only take steps to
counteract it in ourselves and other people. What I'm saying is
you have given your presentation, you've done a great job of it,
but now the audience is jumping to conclusions. They're going to
ask some questions to complete the pattern they've developed in
their own mind about what you have. Why do we chase pattern
making and try to create order out of randomness or chaos,
confusion, or new ideas? Because when we solve a problem we
get an internal reward.
The audience has given you a lot of attention and they want a
reward for listening and giving you all their mental attention. That
reward is in the form of dopamine. We're not going to spend a lot
of time here talking about drugs, but this is important. Dopamine
is the drug of the mind that gives you a high, a soft euphoria when
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	7
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 23 --

you get something you want or when you figure out a puzzle. With
your pitch you are creating a certain desire. That's the job of it: to
make somebody want something. Once we're faced with a desire,
a puzzle, a thing to work out, we can no longer, as humans, sit
idle. We aggressively chase it. We want to know what's knowable.
We want answers. That's where questions are going to come from.
We want that dopamine kick. Passively learning about something
in a partial way is not enough for humans. We want to own it or
we want to know about it completely and we want definitive
answers. Why? Because we're pattern making machines and
when we complete a pattern and solve it, we get a dopamine kick.
We get a high. We get a drug injected into our mind and it feels
great. Euphoric.
When you challenge someone and you create a problem and then
you expose your solution, they want to know if that solution is
real, if it will work for them and if the economics will work out.
They want facts because they want to solve the problem you have
posed. They want to solve the Rubik's cube and until it is solved in
their mind, they are not happy. They know when they do solve it,
they get a dopamine kick. Boom. It feels good. Let's talk a little
more about dopamine. If you want to learn more, there's a
researcher named Peter Diane at the University of College London.
He really studies this stuff. There's three things. When you get
what you expect to get, you get little or no dopamine kick. This is
important because once people are at the end of the presentation
and they get all the information from you and there's nothing
more to get, there's no fix waiting for them. If they knew halfway
through, or three quarters through, what the rest of the
presentation was basically going to be about and they guessed it
correctly, they get little or no dopamine kick. They got what they
expected. Number two, an unexpected gain of money or
information or reward really fires up the dopamine delivery
system. This reward is much stronger than getting something you
expected to get. It's surprise. Surprise is a real reward.
Just a word on money, of course, you aren't genetically
programmed to be excited about money or be surprised when you
think you're going to make some from an investment or buying a
product or getting a windfall. You aren't genetically programmed
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	8
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 23 --

to acknowledge what money is. There's no chip in your brain,
there's no module in your brain that is activated by a financial
reward. Instead, your brain treats potential investing or profits as
part of a broad class of basic rewards: food, drink, safety, sex,
drugs, shelter, music. Even great smells, beautiful faces, fun
social interactions. A financial gain is the most modern member
of this ancient group of feel good experiences. We find out very
early in our lives that money is essential to providing pleasures.
The reflexive part of the human brain responds to potential
financial gains at least as powerfully as it does to the prospects of
capturing more fundamental rewards, such as drugs, music, food.
Even though money cannot satisfy any primary need, because
you can't eat it or mate with it, the association between money
and reward is very strong.
This is why an unexpected gain of money or information that will
lead to making money really fires up the dopamine delivery
system. Again, on the subject of Dopamine, getting what you
expected produces little or no kick. It doesn't give the mind a fix
and it doesn't feel good. An unexpected gain of money or
believing that we're going to make money or get a great product,
get more value than we're paying, really fires up the dopamine
delivery system. That's the second thing about dopamine. The
third thing to remember is that people hate randomness. If your
presentation is confusing in any way they try to solve that
randomness with their own pattern fix. They will say, "I know
what this is." This is a problem. If you did a great job and you gave
them all the information as perfectly as it can be given they say "I
know what this is. This is great," and they're done. They have all
the information they need. There's no dopamine feeding into their
minds. They're no fix coming to them and the meeting is over. I
don't care if the meeting has gone on for 20 minutes and it's
booked for two hours.
You give them all the information they need, you complete the
pattern, you solve the problem, there's no more dopamine.
There's no more drug fix. There's no euphoria coming. The
meeting is over. On the other side, if you've created a problem
they can't solve, they will start to pattern make and try to solve it
themselves with the limited information they have. How do you
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	9
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 23 --

win? You did a great job and the meeting's over, you did not a
great job, they're going to pattern make and solve the problem
themselves with the little information they have and the meeting
is also over. How can this be? We're only 20 minutes into it and
the meeting is over. If you feed into either of these problems, you
give them all the answers, it doesn't matter how hard you try to
regain their attention. It's over. You pack your bags and you go
home.
There's clearly a solution to this or I wouldn't be talking about it.
You have got to help them discover a new and bigger and better
problem. So they can earn a larger and better award for their
mind and get a kick of dopamine. Now, in "Reveal the Intrigue",
you have to open a new area of inquiry that's related to your
pitch. You need to create arousal at this point. We need to
activate the anticipation circuits of the mind. Anticipation
circuitry forces us to pay close attention to the possibility of
coming awards. All this background is leading us to understand
that at the end of the pitch they're tired, they are solving the
problem you have posed and they're basically ready to get out of
the meeting. You've got to recapture attention. Of course, this
module is "R: Reveal the Intrigue" and you can tell by now we're
going to recapture attention by dropping some news intrigue on
them.
As a quick pause, there are some other ways to capture attention
that just works the way the mind likes to work. If you introduce
high contrast ideas in a physical way, they will pay attention to
you. Perception is not passive. If you want people to see clearly,
give them the chance to move around it and see how it interacts
with other objects, people and ideas. Remember, we're very good
at interpolating, getting small pieces of information and making
patterns and coming up with answers from multiple sources.
We're not going to talk about specifically how to do that, but
introducing high contrast ideas in a physical way will get people
paying attention to you. A second thing you can do is introduce an
object. Objects that abruptly appear get really fast attention. In
general, our brain gives special treatment to objects as opposed
to backgrounds and shadows and landscape.
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	10
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 23 --

Think about it in an evolutionary way. People or animals or food
require response of some kind from the animal within us. If you
introduce an object you're going to capture attention at this
point. I'm not proposing you do that. I'm not going to give you a
method to do that. It's just something to think about in context. A
third thing you can do, is if you introduce agency and motion,
animacy, as it's called. People will respond to it. We evolved in a
world where animals and objects had to be paid attention to.
Living things are more difficult and dangerous objects than a rock.
Our brains are biased in lots of ways to detect movement, or what
scientists call agency, things that happen because someone
wanted them to happen and have to be paid attention to. Anytime
you can introduce a sense of motion or agency to your pitch or
your product or idea, you're going to recapture attention. It's a far
bigger subject than we can address here, but it's important to
recognize. High contrast ideas, objects that abruptly appear and
human-like or animal-like motion get attention.
I just mentioned that because I now want to move into the last bit
of context before we start doing some very specific things to
develop intrigue. The reason we're so deeply in the subject of
intrigue is that look at it this way: one, we have just given a pitch.
Two, people are tired. Three, they are starting to make
assumptions about the value of what we have. Four, they are
asking questions to validate their assumptions. For other reasons,
too, but a lot of those questions are going to come to validate the
assumptions they are making. Five, they are craving a dopamine
hit and we have not given them a new way to get a dopamine
kick. Six, the meeting is coming to an end quickly and this is a
problem. The cause of this problem is that people perceive
patterns where none exist, as we've said before. People leap to
conclusions from fragments of information.
It is important to understand how this came about. Why do we
have this inside of us? I want to say a little bit about our history
just as a species. We as humans are hunter-gatherers. We lived in
small, nomadic bands. We were looking for people to mate with in
most of our time, or finding shelter and chasing things to eat. We
spent a lot of our time avoiding predators and foraging for fruits,
seeds or roots. Our earliest ancestors, the decisions they faced in
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	11
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 23 --

daily life were very new and not very complex. Early man, what
did he have to do? Avoid places where crocodiles and jaguars and
piranha hang out. They had to figure out if it was going to rain for
the day. They had to understand what kind of people would want
to take their food away and try to kill them. If you were early man,
you had to learn how to kill those people after you identified them
and you had to try not to get it wrong very often. These are the
basic tasks our brain evolved to perform: find something to eat,
find something to kill, find something to mate with, avoid piranha,
look for rain, and are the approaching people friends or enemies.
That's it. This is what we evolved to do. We're different from all
other creatures in our ability to forecast, interpret, observe,
correlate and understand cause and effect. Other things on this
Earth don't do that. Our human race is, what, 200,000 years old?
There hasn't been much additional development in that time.
As a quick aside, in 1997, the paleoanthropologists that do this
kind of stuff found a 154,000 year-old Homosapien skull in
Ethiopia. It would have held 1,450 cubic centimeters in volume of
brain. That's the same average of people living today. Not much
development in 200,000 years. It's easy to visualize the ancient
east African plain which we came from. I've said many times that
we have dropped out of the trees into the African savannah. That
was a simple environment with alternating regions of sun and
shade, dense green areas of tree and grass, vast open ground with
small rivers that had dangerous sloping banks and that's it. If this
were a video game, it would be considered one of the worst ever.
Too easy to figure out what to do next. Simple patterns clue you in
this environment how to adapt for survival. Once you get this
environment right, food, shelter, friends, mates, you didn't need
to know a lot more. You didn't go out and collect more
information.
Our ancestors learned to make the most of small samples of data
and today, our brains still work that way. This is what I call "I get
it". Our brains work on very small samples and people say "I get
it". They pattern make and fill in the rest of it. They don't need all
the details to make assumptions. People make decisions on very
small samples of information. As I've said before, they perceive
patterns where none exist and they leap to conclusions from
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	12
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 23 --

fragments of information. We date back 200,000 years as I've
said before. But food, crops and the first cities only date back
11,000 years, 5%. The earliest known financial markets in which
products like rice and metal were traded were from Mesopotamia.
But the more formal markers where the trading of stocks and
financial instruments happen, those are only 400 years old. We
date back 200,000 years but the trading of financial instruments
dates 400 years.
If you could imagine all of human history as your ten mile
commute to work, the first financial transactions would not show
up until your elevator ride to the fifth floor office. This is why our
primitive brain finds economic calculations so difficult to focus on.
It's great for solving prehistoric problems. Simple patterns, strong
emotion responses, it does it fast, instantly and efficiently. We
have 200,000 years of experience doing those things. We only
have 400 years of experience working on economics and financial
transactions. The brain, the mind of the person you're pitching to
is not good at identifying randomness, organizing complex ideas
and making multivariate decisions.
These are challenges our ancestors rarely faced. But your
customer has to do it the whole time he's dealing with you. They
will perceive patterns where none exist. They will leap to
conclusions from fragments of information and that is because
there is 200,000 years of experience built into the mind of the
person you're sitting across, to do that. There is less than 400
years of experience in that person to understand the economic
value proposition you are proposing. You need their attention so
they can slowly understand the economics and the value
proposition of what you have. That takes time to get into their
mind and time requires attention.
Now that we understand why intrigue is so important and what
the evolutionary biology basis is for needing to use intrigue at the
end of the pitch, we can move into applications of it. Why would
you want intrigue? Of course, because the person is tired. They're
starting to make assumptions. They're pattern making and there
are serious problems emerging. They're starting to ask questions
and the social layer is breaking apart. The meeting is nearly over.
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	13
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 23 --

That's why you want to use intrigue. Then, it begs the question,
what makes things intriguing. First of all, let's start with the
basics. You're intriguing. You build celebrity status through your
knowledge, your insight, your value.
As many of you know, I help companies raise money. I'm different
from most guys who do this in this way: once I agree to take on a
deal, I don't give up. I'm like a bulldog on a bone. I'm 35 for 35.
One of those raises I found $6 million in 30 days. That was great.
In another one, it took me six months to find $5 million. I had to
travel to Russia, Switzerland and Italy. In Russia, they do business
very differently and I'd love to talk to you about that when we get
some time together outside of this program. There is a lot of
money in Russia. If you know how to get it, it's pretty easy. It took
me about five months to figure out how it works there but once I
did, it happened quickly. On that deal, I spent more than I made.
That really hurt. But what I have learned is that when you take on
a deal and you tell somebody you're going to do something, then
you follow through to the end. Here's something else I've learned:
sweet are the fruits of labor.
You are an intriguing person. I think if I heard somebody say that
to me, I would be intrigued. You can find your own methodology,
but you've got to dig deep and find out what's interesting about
you without bragging in a weird way and relate it in a social way
that's also economic. You are intriguing. The second thing that's
intriguing is to say "I know we're selling to you, we want your
money, we want your purchase order or we want your
investment, but you actually can't have it until we authorize you."
This is very common. You might say "This transaction is a little
different than some you might be familiar with. We have a pool of
investors. We have five investors in our company and all the
decisions about the company need to be unanimous. So I have to
carefully screen who we add as an investor pool to the company.
Because we need all parties focused on the same financial
outcome. If you have different goals from the other investors
we're not going to be able to make unanimous decisions. Even if
you want to invest and give me a check right now, it's not
possible. I have to screen you and I have to authorize you so the
other investors know that they're working in a peer group so
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	14
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 23 --

when there are financial decisions to be made, you're not going to
be a bad apple or a problem. Yes, we want you to want to be in
our deal, but at the moment that you want it, we still have to get
you authorized.
There's a third way to develop intrigue and it's very common. That
is to say, "We can only sell you our basic product. You cannot have
the premium product." That's actually quite common, right? Many
companies require you to buy the basic product before you can
have the premium product. Think about a snowboarding school.
You can't just sign up for snowboarding and say I want to go on
the jumps and I want to go in the rail park. You've got to
demonstrate that you can get down the bunny slope first. Then
you can get down the blue slopes. Only then can you advance to
do the cool stuff, the stuff the instructors and the advance
students know how to do. You cannot pay them any amount of
money to take you immediately to the rail park because you will
die. They don't want that. You cannot have their premium
product. First you have to buy their basic product.
My own business is the same way. If you want me to raise money
for your deal, we need you to buy our buy our basic advisory
service product. We've got to take you through that program so
you understand how we work. It's only a couple thousand dollars.
You've got to understand how we work. Then if you want to move
through capital raising, we can advance. But we're not going to
jump right into capital raising until you have bought our basic
product because we don't know you well enough. Of course, there
is more product that you haven't seen. You can't buy our
premium product until you buy our basic product. That is always
intriguing. It lets you describe things people can't have. It's very
authentic. As you're building your own version of that, again,
think about the metaphor of the snowboarding school. Nobody is
going to put you in a rail park until you've passed their earlier
classes.
This, number three, wait to develop intrigue, is very powerful. I call
this the "We're not sure we're right for each other yet." This is
similar to "You can't have it," but it's on a relationship basis. "You
can't have it" means we've got to authorize you. "We're not sure
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	15
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 23 --

we're right for each other" is "I haven't authorized your
personality. I'm not sure we would get along." You might say
something like, "We care about relationships with our customers.
There are some things about you that we like, for sure. We
wouldn't stay here and we wouldn't be asking you to buy this. But
there's some things where we're not sure about you. Just because
you're a buyer, it doesn't mean we have to jump into a
relationship with you. There's something called counter-party
risk. Even if you buy from us, you can be a horrible customer and
we can make no profit on you or we could lose money on you. You
have customers yourself like that. I want to do a bit more work to
understand you to make sure that not only is this product going to
be great for you because that's what you want, but that you're
going to be a great customer for us. I'm just not there yet".
How does this work, this idea of counter-party? I'll give you an
example. Let's say you sell MRI machines to hospitals. A hospital
chain wants to order 500, but you don't have 500 MRI machines
in stock. That might be a $50 million inventory. You have to build
those. They better be a damn good customer because if they
change that order after you've started building and have ordered
the raw materials for 500 machines, you could lose millions.
Buyers cannot be automatically customers. You have to think
about them as a counter-party. If they're going to be a counter-
party that can cause you damage, you have to make damn sure
it's a good relationship. Will the customer let you profit on it? Do
they want you to profit? Are they easy to work with? Are they fun
to work with? Are they additive to your company? Are they a good
counter-party?
In the credit markets, if you really want to learn about counter-
parties, go to the internet and type in "credit market counter-
party" and you will see lots of descriptions. Buyers need to be
good counter-party. You can't just take their money if they're bad
people. If they're acting up or they're trying to do things that are
out of phase with the way you run your sales process you can just
say, "I'm not sure we're right for each other and we've got to
explore this. Even though you're buying what we have, you're also
our counter-party and we need to make sure we have a great
relationship."
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	16
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 16 of 23 --

That is very intriguing to people that you would even say that. That
is a great way to develop intrigue. It does other things as well, but
it's great for intrigue. The next point of intrigue is to deliver
certainty. This is counter intuitive. Certainty is very intriguing. Let
me give you an example of Acme Software Company that I was
helping raise money. We were able to offer investors a guaranteed
20% for reason the fund that was already invested in them could
not invest any more money but they wanted to. So if somebody
else invested, they would guarantee them a 20% return back by
$50 million in assets. As much as anything can be a bond life
guarantee, this was it at a 20% return. That was very intriguing. A
lot of people wanted to look at that because there are many deals
that say "We'll give you a 20% return," and there are many deals
that say "We'll guarantee you a 20% return," but there are very
few that say, "We guarantee you a 20% return backed by assets."
That certainty, if you think about it, becomes very intriguing.
In another deal, and I'm going to show you that one specifically
later, we were able to say "Next year's revenue is already under
contract. $30 Million of next year's revenue is already under
contract." Most companies will say, "Next year's projection is $30
million from our existing customers". Very few can say that
revenue is already under contract. That certainty, when we
provided it in the description of the business, is what changed the
face of the deal. Then it became very easy to get meetings.
If you want to think about certainty in an interesting way, we saw
last year a movie called "The Expendables". Look it up. It's an
action movie. It's got Sylvester Stallone, Jason Statham, Jet Li,
Dolph Lungren, Randy Couture, the UFC Fighter, Steve Austin and
Mickey Rourke in it. How can you go wrong? If you like action
movies, that roster gives you certainty that some stuff is going to
blow up, people are going to be shooting things and it's going to
be mayhem. Certainty. That movie cost $80 million to make and it
made $300 million in the box office. Certainty is very intriguing.
There's another kind of certainty that is very powerful that I've
found in my own experience. That's when you add certainty and
celebrity. I will show you this in practice now. Here is a pitch deck
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	17
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 23 --

I built for the Bryan brothers, who are a world famous duo. The
winning-est duo in doubles tennis. They're amazing athletes and
they've got one of the best records of all time. They have been
starting an apparel business. This is the deck we built for them.
What we found is that when we told people about the celebrity
status of these athletes and we showed them their winning track
record, even people that weren't familiar with them. We showed
them they were starting this tennis line of amazing,
performance-oriented tennis apparel, we found that when we
told people about the Bryan brothers and the reach of their
platform and celebrity status, we could also combine it with the
fact that they could call distributors because of their celebrity and
make distributors take their product. This is because their
distributors were their friends and people they knew very well.
If you add certainty and celebrity together then it became very
interesting. There are all kinds of things you can do with celebrity.
You can say "You as an investor in the Bryan brothers business,
we have to screen you. These are world class athletes. They're the
all-American winning guys. They're not just going to take any
investor that's going to be a bad apple. We really have to
understand your experience as an investor and how you can add
value to this business. These guys really care about relationships
so we have to see if the chemistry is good between us." So you
can do all kinds of things when you have certainty and celebrity.
The Bryan brothers have a brand. Your behavior will reflect on
their brand. They really want to make sure that you're the right
investor. Certainly because of their status and their far-reaching
platform and the millions of people that know them all over the
world, the launch of this brand and apparel gear is very certain.
Again, they're just going to call the distributors and say, "We're
shipping you boxes. Put it on the shelf. It's the Bryan brothers."
Adding certainty and celebrity together is very intriguing. People
are obviously attracted to celebrity. They're attracted to certainty.
You put those together and it becomes quite an amazing pull on
people. It gets their attention.
As I mentioned, certainty is a very powerful way to introduce
intrigue. I'm going to show you an example of how I've used that
before. This is a pitch deck I built for a company we raised money
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	18
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 18 of 23 --

for. It was a very difficult subject. It's a genetics complex field.
There's not a lot of investors for it. What we discovered is until we
started providing certainty in the deal, it was hard to get people to
pay enough attention to the overall deal to absorb it. On this page,
the revenue summary is where we talked about the next year's
revenue as being under contract. That seems like an obvious thing
to do, but it wasn't at the time. Once I developed this single page,
it changed the complete finance-ability of the whole company. It
seemed obvious when we said "There are customer contracts,"
but when I built this page like this and said "The next year's
revenue are under contract by these customers", it was intriguing
enough to get people to pay enough attention to the deal to
absorb all the other complex aspects of it. That certainty can be
very intriguing.
As we continue to talk about applications in intrigue, I'm going to
show you another pitch deck that falls under the "You are
intriguing" subject. I think we provide this as part of the Pitch
Mastery program. You can download this and look at it. You are
intriguing. In this case, we built this pitch deck for a wealth
manager. We made the whole deck about Alex and what's
interesting about him. Alex has a very interesting story that this
deck communicates. You'll see that within it. Certainly, when he
gives it in person. He was a paramedic. He ended up saving a
young woman's life. It's an amazing story when you hear him tell
it. He came to really care, because of his family, about the
financial well-being of physicians. He left a career he was very
good at and a career he enjoyed to do this wealth management
for physicians. He's an expert in it. He focuses on it. His family is in
it. He has done some amazing things to celebritize himself. He's
told his company he's not going to take hundreds of clients like
they ask him to do, but just focus on less than 50 clients in a
specific field. This whole deck, instead of being focused on a
company, is focused on an individual. I recommend you go
through it. It's a great example of how to celebritize yourself and
make yourself interesting.
A lot of this module has been about understanding the role of
intrigue in the overall pitch process and why at the end of the
pitch and telling the story, you have to be so focused on
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	19
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 23 --

developing intrigue. If at the beginning, I just said "Hey, at the end
of the pitch, you need to deploy some intrigue," it wouldn't have
been deeply felt. Now, you understand why it's so important. It's
time to talk about action steps and how to actually do it. The first
thing in developing intrigue actually has nothing to do with
intrigue. You have got to be willing to walk away from the deal.
You have got to be willing to walk away from the room or the
phone call or the internet site transaction. You've got to be willing
to walk away inside yourself. If you don't, it's going to be a boring
meeting. Your neediness is going to be cloying. We talked about
this in "Prizing". We talked about this in "Framing". We talked
about this in "Tell the Story". We're talking about it again in
"Reveal the Intrigue". You have got to be willing to walk away. If
you don't have this fundamental skill inside you, everything else is
going to be built like castles on sand. You're not going to have a
strong foundation. A strong foundation is the willingness to walk
away and to signal at many times across the presentation that if
it's not right for you, you will take your prize, your toys, and go
play in a different sand box. It's very important.
I'll give you a quick example. I was pitching to a company on
handling some capital raising for them last week. They asked me
what my fees are. I said, "My fees are reasonable. They tend to
even be below market. But they're at market." I've learned from a
partner of mine many years ago, even though because of my skill
set I can charge high fees higher than anybody else, I've learned
not to be a pig. John would say, "Hey, Oren, don't be a pig." I
would say, "John, we could charge 10% even though market is
7% on the fees for this deal. We can get 3% more than anybody
else can get." He would say, "Oren, don't be a pig. Leave some
money on the table for other people. Don't load the deal. Take a
fair fee and leave money on the table."
I said to the guys I was pitching, when they said, "Oren, what are
you fees", I said, "Well, I learned many years ago from this guy,
John, not to be a pig and we charge you reasonable fees, 5% to
7% on equity and 1% on debt." He said to me "Wow. I negotiate
hard because I'm a pig. I want as much as I can get for myself."
That's what the VP of finance said. I had to signal to him that I was
willing to walk away. I said "If that's how you feel, we would not
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	20
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 20 of 23 --

work well together. I would not take this deal on. If you really felt
that way, that's how you treat deals and you don't mind taking
more than your fair share and you don't want to leave anything on
the table for other people, I would never want to work with you. I
wouldn't want to do this deal." Of course, 10 seconds later he
came back and said, "I'm just kidding. You were talking about
being a pig. I was just kidding about all that. That's not how I am. I
value relationships," and everything else like that. You have to be
able to signal your willingness to walk away. That's the
fundamental action step or foundation to being able to deliver
intrigue or to do any pitch. It's not to be overt about it. You can't
at every minute say, "Hey, I'm willing to walk away. I'm willing to
walk away. If you won't do what I want, I'm willing to walk away".
You've got to signal this willingness at the right time. Especially
when people are out of phase with your values. You can say, "That
wouldn't work for us. This is not a deal we would do."
The next thing you have to do is identify and collect all the
resources you have. By resources, I mean the valuable, insightful
knowledge you have about the market, the product and the idea.
Not stuff that's well-known or in the common body of knowledge.
It's got to be real insight and the insight has to be useful,
actionable and interesting. The reason I'm asking you to collect all
this information and resources is because there's a worksheet
that is going to help you build your intrigue specifically. You need
to know what insightful knowledge you have so that within the
worksheet, you get a specific script on how to reveal things that
are intriguing at the end of your presentation or pitch.
Another thing you want to think about is what risk can a
customer give you. How can a customer be a risky counter-party?
Can they place a giant order? Walmart can be a great customer,
but a risky counter-party. They can order more stuff than you can
manufacture. If you sell toothbrushes and Walmart gives you an
order for 1,500,000 and your suppliers can't get you that much,
you have a problem. What risks can your customers give you?
What problems can your customers cause you? In the worksheet,
you'll see how to take those risks and specifically develop intrigue
out of them.
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	21
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 21 of 23 --

The next thing is what aspects of your product or service are for
power users? If you recall, we said one way to develop intrigue is
to say, "You can't have the part of the product that's for power
users. You have to start with the basic one." In order to figure out
how to provide that intrigue, you have to answer the question
"What aspects of the product or service are for power users?".
The next thing you have to decide and think about, this will be in
the worksheet as well, is what signs of incompatibility do you look
for in people? If they're rude, if they want to rush the
presentation, if they're asking too many analytical questions, if
they're moving too slow or too fast, too fat or too skinny, they
don't like you or you don't like them, what signs of incompatibility
do you look for? It's very intriguing to say, "I'm not sure we're
compatible yet. We need to explore that some more." This
worksheet will give you a specific script on how to do that. You
have got to look for those signs of incompatibility.
The next thing is what's cool about what you do? Obviously, things
that are cool are intriguing. Some things I think about are I have a
cool car. When they sold it to me, they said, "There are some
super cool things about this car." The subtext is that it's
intriguing. "I know you'll never probably use it, but at 80 miles per
hour, this wing pops out and it stabilizes the car. It's very cool." At
the point that I was getting cool on buying that car, what did they
do? They said "Let me show you some cool stuff." You've got to
think about your product in terms of what you do, what's cool
about the idea, what's cool about the product. When things start
to cool off at the end of your pitch, you're going to bring that cool
factor in, which is intriguing. The worksheet will give you a specific
script for doing that. But you have to bring the raw material.
Again, step one is identify and collect all your resources. What's
valuable and insightful? What knowledge do you have that's true
insight about your idea or your industry, your product, your
service or your business? Two, what risks can a customer give
you? What counter-party risks can they provide? Three, what
aspects of your product are for power users? What's the
equivalent of the snowboard rail park in your business? Four,
what signs of incompatibility do you look for in other people? It's
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	22
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 22 of 23 --

very intriguing to people when you say, "I'm not sure I would do
business with you even if you handed me a check. We may be
incompatible because of this reason." Five, what's cool about
what you do? I mean really cool, not fake cool.
I'm just going to give you a quick graphic to let you know what's
ahead and where we've come from and where we are and where
we're going. In the broader scheme of things, we set the frame
and we told the story because we wanted attention. At the end of
the pitch, some things are happening that we have to be
concerned about. We've given the pitch and people are tired.
They're starting to make assumptions about the value of what we
have. They are now going to ask questions. These questions are
going to destroy or break down the social layer. They're asking
questions to validate the assumptions they're making about you.
The audience, the buyer, the people you're pitching to are craving
another dopamine fix and we haven't given them away at the end
of the pitch. We're going to give them away to get a new
dopamine kick. The meeting is coming to an end and that's a
problem. That's why we have to reveal some intrigue at this point.
Next, you're going to download the worksheet and really find
ways to do that specifically for your business.
We're going to help you to the extent we can. But you're going to
start with the worksheet. What's coming next is how to offer the
prize and that's very exciting because now you'll be moving from
engagement into decision. Certainly when we nail the hook point,
we're going to go to decision mode and get the deal, it's obviously
when money transacts. There's still work to do to get money to
transact. Even if you've created the great desire, they want the
product, you have nailed the hook point, you've still got to get
money to transact. We still have some work to do, but we're very
far from getting a deal closed. Download the worksheet now and
we'll see you on the next module, which is "Offer the Prize".
R: Reveal the Intrigue
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	23
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 23 of 23 --
